title: Operationalizing climate justice in the implementation of Boston's Building Performance Standard
data_sources: participant observation in City of Boston Environment Department

data_scope.study_design: qualitative mixed-methods case study (single city, single policy)
data_scope.policy: BERDO (Building Emissions Reduction and Disclosure Ordinance)

core_conclusion: Translating climate justice from a planning ideal into a working building-emissions regulation is politically contested rather than technical. An ethnography of Boston's BERDO rule-making shows justice is reshaped by scope-and-scale discrepancies, bureaucratization, and weaponization, where actors invoke justice to resist or redirect policy, revealing why operationalizing climate justice on the ground is far harder than committing to it.
why_it_matters: Opens the implementation black box that most climate-justice work ignores; buildings are the frontier of urban decarbonization and BERDO sets precedent for other US cities; names concrete failure modes (scope/scale, bureaucratization, weaponization); rare 5-month insider access to the implementing agency.
why_nature: Transferable governance lessons from a single rich case; couples social/political process with built-environment carbon policy; strongly policy-translatable for equitable Building Performance Standards; methodologically distinctive depth of access.
added_value_to_my_research: Geocode scope/scale discrepancies onto EJ + housing layers -> spatial equity exposure map for building policy
Train a discourse classifier on coded comment text -> scalable detection of weaponized equity rhetoric
Link rule-making to air-quality and health outcomes -> building-decarbonization to health-accessibility chain
Agent-based / Monte-Carlo green-gentrification stress test of retrofit mandates
Release a FAIR de-identified governance codebook -> comparative cross-city policy-process database

related.relation: procedural justice gap mirrors BERDO's contested rule-making

related.title: Early engagement and co-benefits strengthen cities' climate commitments
related.relation: engagement design relevant to BERDO's CAG process and reproducible comparison
